summary: Gruber dissects Joe Wilcox's argument that the iPhone 'cannot win the smartphone wars,' pointing out that Wilcox defines 'winning' solely as highest unit sales volume — a metric Apple has never prioritized. Gruber argues that measuring by profits and growth instead makes the iPhone look dominant. He challenges the reliance on Gartner projections, noting their poor track record, and points out that iPhone OS market share analyses exclude the iPod Touch. He also argues that the economics of carrier plans make it difficult for competitors to undercut the iPhone on price. Gruber concludes that Wilcox is working backwards from a predetermined conclusion that the iPhone will remain a niche product.
key_points: Defining 'winning' as unit sales volume misses Apple's actual strategy of optimizing for profits and growth
The term 'smartphone' is poorly defined and excludes devices like the iPod Touch that run the same OS
Gartner's track record on market predictions is unreliable, making their 2012 projections suspect
Monthly carrier plan costs are the dominant expense, making it hard for competitors to undercut iPhone on total price
Wilcox appears to start with the conclusion that iPhone will be a niche product and works backwards to support it
money_quotes: I don't think the iPhone, or any Apple product, will ever 'win' in terms of unit sales. But that's because Apple doesn't even want to win that game.
If you instead measure by profits and growth, Apple and the iPhone look like the front-running favorites to me.
I try my best to shy away from the term 'smartphone', because I don't know what it means exactly.
What we're talking about are handheld networked computing devices.
The only way for a competitor to undercut the iPhone on price would be for some carrier to undercut AT&T on monthly service plan rates. Don't hold your breath.
If you look at the evidence and the direction of the trend lines, the iPhone's prospects look stunning.
I think Wilcox is instead starting with the conclusion that the iPhone will never break out of a Mac-sized market share niche, and working backwards from there.
key_insight: Market share measured by unit sales is the wrong metric for evaluating Apple's success — profits, growth, and the total ecosystem of devices running iPhone OS tell a very different story.
